→ Why sharp reversals suddenly develop during quiet sessions
→ Why headline-driven moves can stall, reverse, or extend further than expected
→ Why a company can beat earnings and raise guidance — and still see its stock decline

See you there.

tweets.text:
Huge respect to the @ResolvLabs team,

I remember when the Resolv exploit happened back in March, and I posted that the best decision might be to just wait.

It paid off. I claimed 100% of the value of my USR.

TL;DR:

So basically, if you held USR before the exploit, the plan says you get 1 USR = 1 USDC back, so those users are mostly protected.

BUT (!) If you bought USR after the exploit, recovery is lower because those tokens may have been mixed with the illicitly minted supply.

Sucks that not everyone gets the same outcome, but at least there’s now a clear framework instead of users being left in limbo.

Good to see them putting a path forward on the table.

BUT (!) If you bought USR after the exploit, recovery is lower because those tokens may have been mixed with the illicitly minted supply.

Sucks that not everyone gets the same outcome, but at least there’s now a clear framework instead of users being left in limbo.

Good to see them putting a path forward on the table.

tweets.text:
Bear markets are for building.

It might sound cliché, but it's true.
Not many people do it during periods of low activity, so it's a perfect time to stand out with your content.

These 15 accs kept posting quality content throughout this market cycle:

→ @DefiIgnas
→ @intern
→ @Defi_Warhol
→ @the_smart_ape
→ @TheDeFiPlug
→ @mztacat
→ @Hercules_Defi
→ @onchainmo
→ @thelearningpill
→ @Kaffchad
→ @tomwanhh
→ @MerlinEgalite
→ @diegoxyz
→ @moonshiesty

Following the right accs makes all the difference for your timeline.

had a good call with @AlchemistTheOG and @AdamWeitsman 

alchemist recently joined bayc and didn't receive a warm reception.  i explained why:

(1) past relationship with lies about the club
(2) receiving an ape from someone who already had dubious intentions to launch an extractive shitcoin

with this post, he's signaling a shift, and that he's ready for a reset and a clean slate

so from here on out, i'm asking all sides to chill out

a lot of work ahead, let's all row in the same direction

tweets.text:
Onchain TCGs and collectibles are the better version of what NFTs always wanted to be.

And the best part is that it’s a far more sustainable market.

It’s much easier to onboard non-crypto users around things they already know and genuinely love, rather than trying to convince them to spend 6-figures on a JPEG of an ape.

@Collector_Crypt alone is now processing more volume than the entire NFT market combined, not to mention individual platforms like OpenSea or Magic Eden, which were once valued in the billions.

Decacorn in the making.

The market might be a dumpster fire right now but if you’re in Australia or NZ and want a free $30 in BTC then boy do I have good news for you!

My friends at Swyftx have hooked me up with a special sign up code that lets anyone who creates a new account get $30 in BTC no strings attached

And really it feel like more than $30 cause like if you hold for long enough it might be worth $300, or better yet, sell the BTC and buy TAO or something else AI related that tickles your fancy (I’m maybe a bit bullish on the AI coins)

I’ve been an ambassador with Swyftx for over 6 months now and it’s been so awesome to work with the team on a whole host of fun initiatives

Not only is their product S tier but more importantly imo all the people that work there are S tier

Sign up here: https://t.co/sjo6u1Euvs

(If you’re not in Aus or NZ then go touch grass, being on CT during a market dumpster fire ain’t no good for you)

tweets.text:
Updates since then:

* Deepseek v4 is out. There *is* a 2-bit quant that can run within 90 GB ( https://t.co/yM1HMZXkXn ), and it works, however it's only fast on Apple hardware (I've head ~35 tok/s). On AMD, it's ~7 tok/s. IMO actually taking the effort to properly support more than one hardware manufacturer is a great example of the difference between mere "decentralized AI" and genuine "CROPS AI". I hope we can become better at this.
* https://t.co/CFYF1smBH3 also has alpha telegram support now. However, the path to adding your account is quite janky
* https://t.co/za4h233eYz looks promising as a way to run "dense" models (eg. Qwen 27B) more efficiently. It's janky, but on my 5090 laptop it seems to be ~2x more tok/s than llama.cpp
* VoxTerm (local AI recording, no third-party servers) continues to be developed https://t.co/GSdKzkD9Ql

And there's a lot more projects coming on the horizon.

One other thing that has been on my mind is that there's actually a lot of intersection between "CROPS ethereum access layer" and "CROPS AI". For example, we want a ZK way to make (paid) calls to remote LLMs. But if we have this, then it's just as useful for solving another problem: private RPC reads in Ethereum.

Another example: application-specific finetuned LLMs. Leanstral ( https://t.co/ilfww8ekJu ; I get ~38 tok/s on AMD) fits into < 70 GB, but can hold its own against 1T models on writing Lean code. Things like this are a huge boon for writing more secure code ( https://t.co/6YPWgVSzCg ). We should have models finetuned for Ethereum-related use cases as well.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
My self-sovereign / local / private / secure LLM setup, April 2026

https://t.co/zmG8wrg7pQ
tweets.full_text:
Updates since then:

* Deepseek v4 is out. There *is* a 2-bit quant that can run within 90 GB ( https://t.co/yM1HMZXkXn ), and it works, however it's only fast on Apple hardware (I've head ~35 tok/s). On AMD, it's ~7 tok/s. IMO actually taking the effort to properly support more than one hardware manufacturer is a great example of the difference between mere "decentralized AI" and genuine "CROPS AI". I hope we can become better at this.
* https://t.co/CFYF1smBH3 also has alpha telegram support now. However, the path to adding your account is quite janky
* https://t.co/za4h233eYz looks promising as a way to run "dense" models (eg. Qwen 27B) more efficiently. It's janky, but on my 5090 laptop it seems to be ~2x more tok/s than llama.cpp
* VoxTerm (local AI recording, no third-party servers) continues to be developed https://t.co/GSdKzkD9Ql

And there's a lot more projects coming on the horizon.

One other thing that has been on my mind is that there's actually a lot of intersection between "CROPS ethereum access layer" and "CROPS AI". For example, we want a ZK way to make (paid) calls to remote LLMs. But if we have this, then it's just as useful for solving another problem: private RPC reads in Ethereum.

Another example: application-specific finetuned LLMs. Leanstral ( https://t.co/ilfww8ekJu ; I get ~38 tok/s on AMD) fits into < 70 GB, but can hold its own against 1T models on writing Lean code. Things like this are a huge boon for writing more secure code ( https://t.co/6YPWgVSzCg ). We should have models finetuned for Ethereum-related use cases as well.

tweets.text:
David Sacks’ Most Contrarian Take for 2026: AI Will Create MORE White Collar Jobs, Not Destroy Them

From All-In's 2026 Predictions Show, Published January 9th

@Jason:

“Sacks, you got a contrarian belief for 2026?”

@DavidSacks:

“Yes. AI will increase demand for knowledge workers, not decrease it.

I would refer you to Aaron Levie's post called Jevons Paradox for Knowledge Workers, and the point of Jevons Paradox is that as the cost of a resource goes down, the aggregate demand for it actually increases because you discover more and more use cases.

So I think this will certainly happen with code. In the past, it's been very expensive to generate code. You have to hire engineers, there's not enough of them, it's an expensive resource. So the amount of software generated in the economy was limited by that.

I think it's going to increase massively now because the cost of generating code is coming down so much.

But there's other examples, too.

You take a field like radiology, that's frequently cited as a profession that AI is going to put out of business. That's not what the data shows. The data shows that the number of radiologists is increasing. Why? Because the number of scans that people want to make is increasing.

And it's true that AI can do some of the work, but you still need a doctor to prompt the AI, to interpret the AI, to validate it.

So you get more efficient, the cost of scans goes down, and instead of it being a super specialty that happens very rarely, that you need a referral on top of a referral to get, it becomes something that's normalized, and everyone starts doing it, and you start getting more and more scans, that leads to better and better outcomes.

I think there's going to be a lot of those examples through the economy, and we're going to look back and see that the job loss narrative was not only wrong, but we actually got job gains.”
tweets.full_text:
David Sacks’ Most Contrarian Take for 2026: AI Will Create MORE White Collar Jobs, Not Destroy Them

From All-In's 2026 Predictions Show, Published January 9th

@Jason:

“Sacks, you got a contrarian belief for 2026?”

@DavidSacks:

“Yes. AI will increase demand for knowledge workers, not decrease it.

I would refer you to Aaron Levie's post called Jevons Paradox for Knowledge Workers, and the point of Jevons Paradox is that as the cost of a resource goes down, the aggregate demand for it actually increases because you discover more and more use cases.

So I think this will certainly happen with code. In the past, it's been very expensive to generate code. You have to hire engineers, there's not enough of them, it's an expensive resource. So the amount of software generated in the economy was limited by that.

I think it's going to increase massively now because the cost of generating code is coming down so much.

But there's other examples, too.

You take a field like radiology, that's frequently cited as a profession that AI is going to put out of business. That's not what the data shows. The data shows that the number of radiologists is increasing. Why? Because the number of scans that people want to make is increasing.

And it's true that AI can do some of the work, but you still need a doctor to prompt the AI, to interpret the AI, to validate it.

So you get more efficient, the cost of scans goes down, and instead of it being a super specialty that happens very rarely, that you need a referral on top of a referral to get, it becomes something that's normalized, and everyone starts doing it, and you start getting more and more scans, that leads to better and better outcomes.

I think there's going to be a lot of those examples through the economy, and we're going to look back and see that the job loss narrative was not only wrong, but we actually got job gains.”

tweets.text:
I believe people are miscontextualizing what it means for companies to cut back on AI costs because of "no meaningful work"

The fact is, most of this AI usage comes from middle management boomers who understand nothing about how to use this technology

IMO its why I say that - while the knee jerk reaction is replacing juniors because you "don't need junior work", the correct reaction is to actually replace the people that "don't know AI" - and the juniors who grew up in the Internet Era definitely know how to use AI

The world is split into those who don't know AI, and those who do
